summary:
The invention of the wheel, occurring approximately 3500–3000 BC in the region of Mesopotamia and the Pontic steppe, is among the most consequential technological developments in human history. The earliest evidence for wheeled vehicles comes from pictographs in ancient Sumer (modern Iraq) and from actual wheel-and-axle artefacts found in Flintbek, Germany and other sites dating to around 3400–3000 BC. The wheel transformed transportation, agriculture, and warfare across the ancient world in ways whose effects are still felt in modern technology.

The earliest wheels appear to have developed from the potter's wheel and from the use of rollers to move heavy objects. The innovation of attaching a fixed axle to a disc-shaped wheel, and mounting a box or platform above it to create a wheeled vehicle, was conceptually straightforward but required considerable craft skill to produce reliably. Early wheels were solid discs cut from planks, heavy but functional for the hauling of goods on relatively flat terrain. The spoked wheel, which was lighter and more efficient, appeared later around 2000 BC and transformed chariot warfare.

The social and economic impacts of wheeled transport were enormous. Wheeled ox-carts allowed the movement of bulk agricultural produce across distances that would previously have required impractical quantities of human porterage. They enabled the construction of large monuments — the pyramids of Egypt and the megaliths of Europe depended partly on sledge and possibly wheeled transport for moving heavy stones. Military applications — the war chariot — would transform warfare from the second millennium BC onward. The wheel also contributed to the spread of the [[event:1782|tin bronze trade networks]] and other long-distance commerce by reducing the cost of overland transport.
